12 || JESSIE EVANS, No. CGC 08-473864
. 13 Plaintiff,
4 v. SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT
15
THE UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO, (Filed Pursuant to Court Order of
16 June 15, 2010)
Defendant.
17 /
18 Comes now plaintiff JESSIE EVANS and alleges the following:
19
PRELIMINARY STATEMENT
20
> 1. Jessie Evans, the former head men’s basketball coach at the University of San
» Francisco (USF), brings this action for damages for breach of contract and breach of the covenant of
23 | good faith and fair dealing, based upon (a) USF’s unlawful removal of Evans from his position as
24 | head coach, (b) USF’s breach of confidentiality by making damaging public statements about
25 Evans, and (c) USF’s final, unlawful termination of Evans’ employment contract. Although
26 . . Lo.
Evans’ contract provides for his termination without cause and for USF’s payment of liquidated
27
Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864
Second Amended Complaint - |

 
1 | damages upon such occurrence, defendant USF falsely claimed that it had just cause to terminate his
2 contract in order to avoid making payments required by his contract. Additionally, defendant USF
made false statements about Evans’ job performance and alleged violations of National Collegiate
4
Athletic Association (NCAA) rules, thereby interfering with his ability to obtain new employment as
5
6 a college basketball coach.
7 JURISDICTION AND VENUE
8 2. Plaintiff Evans’ claims arise under the statutory and common law of the State of
9} California.
10 3. The actions and events giving rise to this lawsuit occurred in the City and County of
11
San Francisco.
12
PARTIES
13
14 4, At times relevant hereto, plaintiff JESSIE EVANS was the Head Basketball Coach of
15 | USF’s intercollegiate men’s basketball team. Evans is a resident of the County of Contra Costa.
16 5. At all times relevant hereto, defendant UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO was and
171 is a California non-profit corporation with its principal place of business in the City and County of
18 San Francisco.
19
STATEMENT OF FACTS
20
54 6. Jessie Evans is a well-respected college basketball coach who served as an assistant
yy coach for NCAA Division 1 men’s basketball programs at University of Minnesota, San Diego State,
23 | University of Wyoming, University of Texas, and University of Arizona from 1976 through 1997.
24 7. Evans became the head coach at the University of Louisiana-Lafayette in 1997 and led
25 his team to the NCAA Tournament in 2000 and 2003. Evans left the University of Louisiana-
26 ws .
Lafayette voluntarily to accept the position of head men’s basketball coach at USF. At the time he
27
Second Amended Complaint - 2

 
1 | left the University of Louisiana-Lafayette Evans was in good standing and had good cause to believe
2 | that he could have remained in that position for as long as he wished.
3 8. In 2004, USF Executive Director of Athletics Bill Hogan recruited Evans to be the
Head Basketball Coach at USF. In dosing so he falsely assured Evans that he could look forward to a
6 long career at USF and that USF would scrupulously observe all terms of its Employment Agreement
7 | with Evans, including the five-year initial period of employment set forth therein.
8 9. In light of USF’s representations and in reasonable reliance upon its assurances that it
9 | would honor its contractual commitments to him, Evans resigned from his position at the University
10 of Louisiana-Lafayette, in Lafayette, Louisiana, and relocated to San Francisco with his wife.
" 10... On April 21, 2004, USF and Evans entered into an Employment Agreement whereby
. Evans would serve as the Head Coach of USF’s men’s intercollegiate basketball team for five years,
14 through April 20, 2009. USF agreed to pay Evans a base salary of $200,000 per year, plus (a) annual
15| increases to be determined by the Executive Director of Athletics; (b) standard USF fringe benefits,
16} including health insurance, life insurance, disability, vacation, and retirement plans; (c) housing
17} assistance and moving expenses; (d) an automobile, including liability and comprehensive insurance;
18 (e) reimbursement for out of pocket expenses; (f) term life insurance in the amount of one million
” dollars; (g) bonuses for selection to the NCAA tournament and for each victory in that tournament;
. (h) revenue sharing for men’s basketball season ticket sales in excess of 800; (i) opportunities to earn
» outside income; (j) a guarantee of compensation for radio and television appearances of at least
23 | $25,000 annually; and (k) use of USF’s facilities without charge for summer basketball camps and
24 | clinics. The total annual value of USF’s contract with Evans is over $300,000.
25 11. — Article 1(a) of the Employment Agreement required that both USF and Evans
26 keep the terms of the agreement “strictly confidential.” USF and Evans agreed that
27
Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864ss—SsS
Second Amended Complaint - 3

 
1 | “disagreements between the parties over any term or condition in this agreement shall be
2) treated with confidentiality and that the parties and their representatives shall not publicize to
3 third persons... the fact of a disagreement.”
12.‘ Article 8 of the Employment Agreement between USF and Evans provides for USF’s
6 right to terminate the agreement under “certain limited circumstances.”
7 13. Article 8(b) of the Employment Agreement provides that “just cause” for the
8 || termination of Evans’ employment means:
9 (a) “Deliberate and serious violations of Coach’s duties or refusal or unwillingness to
10 perform such duties in good faith and to the best of Coach’s ability;
M (b) “Material violations by Coach of any of the other terms and conditions of this
. Agreement not remedied after thirty (30) days written notice thereof to Coach;
14 (c) “Coach’s conviction or admission (including a plea of nolo contendere) of any
15 | criminal statute (excluding vehicular misdemeanors), Coach’s confirmed act of moral turpitude,
16|| and/or a confirmed violation by Coach of any rule, regulation, constitutional provision, or bylaw of
17 the NCAA which occurred during the term of this Agreement or prior hereto during any employment
8 by Coach at another NCAA member institution, any of which, in the sole judgment of University
” would reflect adversely upon the University or its athletic program;
, (d) “A serious and intentional violation by Coach of any law, rule, regulation,
2 constitutional provision, or bylaw of the University, the WCC or the NCAA, which in the sole
23 || judgment of University would reflect adversely upon the University or its athletic program, including
24 | any serious violations which may result in the University being placed on probation by the WCC or
25 | the NCAA and including any violation which may have occurred during prior employment by Coach
26 at another NCAA institution;
27
Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864 i ssts—s—S
Second Amended Complaint - 4

 
1 (e) “A serious and intentional violation of any material law, rule, regulation,

2 | constitutional provision, or bylaw of the University, the WCC or the NCAA by a member of the

3 coaching staffer (sic) any other person under Coach’s supervision and direction, including student-

athletes in the intercollegiate men’s basketball program which violation(s) are known to Coach, or

6 should have been known to Coach, and Coach fails to report said violation(s) to the University and, in

7 || turn said failure of Coach may, in the sole but reasonable judgment of University, reflect adversely

8 | upon the University or its athletic program; or

9 (f) “Prolonged unexcused absence from duty.”
10 14. Termination of its Employment Agreement with Evans for cause relieves USF of its
a further obligations to provide him with compensation and related benefits.
15. | Atno time during Evans’ employment at USF did he commit acts that would justify
14 his termination “for cause” under his Employment Agreement with USF.
15 16. Article 1] of the Employment Agreement provides that, “University shall have the
16] right to terminate this Agreement prior to its scheduled expiration on April 20, 2009, without cause.”
17} In such event, “Coach shall be entitled to liquidated damages” pursuant to Article 12, which provides
18 that USF shall pay Coach “an amount equal to Coach’s base salary due under Section 7(a) of this
" Agreement, as increased under Section 7(b), through the scheduled expiration date of this Agreement,
' as may be amended from time to time in accordance with the terms hereof.”
» 17. During Evans’ first season at USF, his team compiled a record of 17 wins, 14 losses,
33 || and was selected to the National Invitation Tournament.
24 18. Evans’ career record as a head coach at USF and at Louisiana-Lafayette through the
25 | 2006-07 season was 159 wins, 129 losses (.552).
26
27

Second Amended Complaint - 5

 
I 19. On May 31, 2005, in recognition of Evans’ “exemplary” performance of his duties and
21 his “success with the men’s basketball team,” USF extended his Employment Agreement to April 20,
3 2010, and increased his compensation by amending the provision on Season Ticket Revenue Sharing
to state the following: “In addition to Coach’s annual base salary, University agrees to pay Coach
6 twenty five percent (25%) of all revenue on season ticket sales in excess of the 2003-04 season
7 | amount ($110,000).”
8 20. On May 15, 2006, USF Executive Athletic Director Bill Hogan evaluated Evans’ job
9| performance. Hogan graded Evans as “Outstanding” or “Exceeds Expectations” on 23 out of 35
10 performance categories and gave him no “Unsatisfactory” ratings.
" 21. On December 26, 2007, the day after Christmas, USF Executive Athletic Director
. Debra Gore-Mann presented Evans with an ultimatum: either (1) “request a leave of absence for the
14 remainder of season to handle a pressing personal or family issue, or other reason;” or (2) suffer an
15 || immediate, mid-season suspension from employment, and the commencement of proceedings to
16 || terminate his employment “for just cause.”
17 22. Faced with this coercive and unacceptable choice between falsely stating that he had
18 requested a leave of absence or facing the humiliating and career damaging prospect of a public
" suspension from his duties in the midst of the basketball season, and following discussions between
his agents and USF Attorney Michael Vartain, Evans reluctantly agreed to issuance of the statement:
» “Coach Jessie Evans has requested a leave for the remainder of the basketball season.” This statement
23 | is not true.
24 23. | On December 26, 2007, Gore-Mann publicly announced in a USF press release to the
25 San Francisco Chronicle and other media that Evans had requested a leave from his duties as head
26
27
Second Amended Complaint - 6

 
1 | coach of the USF men’s basketball program. This statement was false and misleading in that it
2 suggested that Evans had done so voluntarily rather than as a result of coercion.
3
24. On December 26, 2007, USF and Gore-Mann announced that Eddie Sutton would
4
replace Evans as USF’s head basketball coach for the remainder of the 2007-08 season.
5
6 25. Onor about March 10, 2008, USF Attorney Michael Vartain publicly stated in an
7|| article published in the San Francisco Chronicle that Evans was guilty of numerous “secondary”
8 || NCAA violations amounting to the “loss of control” of the men’s basketball program at USF.
9 26. On March 17, 2008, Gore-Mann advised Evans that USF was terminating his
10 employment for “just cause.”
11
12 FIRST CLAIM FOR RELIEF — BREACH OF CONTRACT
B (Violations of contractual provisions for termination and payment of liquidated damages)
14 27. Plaintiff refers to and incorporates by reference paragraphs 1-26 above as though fully
15 || set forth herein.
16 28. By virtue of the foregoing, USF violated plaintiff Evans’ rights under his Employment
171 Agreement with USF by, among other things, terminating his employment without just cause and by
8 failing to compensate him as required by the Agreement in the event of its termination without just
19
cause.
20
2] SECOND CLAIM FOR RELIEF — BREACH OF THE COVENANT OF GOOD FAITH
2 AND FAIR DEALING
23 29. Plaintiff refers to and incorporates by reference paragraphs |-28 above as though fully
24 | set forth herein.
25 30. By virtue of the foregoing, USF violated the covenant of good faith and fair dealing in
26 . . .
its Employment Agreement with Evans through the actions of its agents, Gore-Mann and Vartain, in
27
Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864
Second Amended Complaint - 7

 
1 | coercing Evans to state that he had requested a leave of absence and by making the public statements
21 described above.
3
4 THIRD CLAIM FOR RELIEF —- BREACH OF CONTRACT
(Violations of contractual provision for confidentiality)
5
6 31. —‘~Plaintiff refers to and incorporates by reference paragraphs 1-30 above as
7| though fully set forth herein.
8 32. _ By virtue of the foregoing, USF violated plaintiff Evans’ rights under his
9] Employment Agreement with USF by making damaging public statements about Evans and the
10 nature of his separation from USF, in violation of the Agreement’s confidentiality provision.
11
12 DAMAGES
13 33.  Asaresult of the actions of defendants, Evans has been injured and has suffered
14 .
damages as follows:
15
a. He has been deprived of compensation and other economic benefits to which
16
"7 he is entitled under his Employment Agreement with USF;
18 b. He is likely to lose compensation to which he would otherwise be entitled in
19 the future;
20 c. His reputation in the collegiate basketball community has been damaged; and
21 d. His prospects for future employment and career advancement have been
22 diminished.
23
24 WHEREFORE, plaintiff JESSIE EVANS requests that this Court grant him relief as follows:
25 (1) Damages for past and future lost wages, earnings, and benefits according to proof}
2 ys
6 (2) Interest on damages at the prevailing legal rate;
27
Evans v. The University of San Francisco, No. CGC 08-473864
Second Amended Complaint - 8

 
i (3) Costs of suit; and
2 (4) Such other and further relief as the Court may deem proper.
3 Dated: June 23, 2010
